Canadian Provinces, Territories Agree to First-of-Its-Kind Pact to Drive Connected Electricity Systems
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart


OTTAWA--Governments across Canada are banding together for the first time to connect and bolster the country's independent electricity systems in an effort to boost growth and support development projects.

An agreement between most of Canada's provinces and territories aims to drive new electricity-transmission projects and link a range of energy resources in a move the governments and local providers hope will break down longstanding barriers between provincial grids and allow for a more connected and resilient system.

The drive comes at a time of heightened global trade and geopolitical uncertainty, which has pushed Canada to work to bring down interprovincial trade barriers while it also seeks out new markets beyond an increasingly protectionist U.S.

The national energy-corridor agreement was initiated by Ontario and brings it together with British Columbia, Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba, New Brunswick, Prince Edward Island, Nova Scotia, Yukon and Northwest Territories.

Under the pact, the provinces and territories plan to collaborate to identify and push ahead with new interprovincial and territorial transmission infrastructure, including key high-voltage, long-distance transmission interconnection developments between neighboring electric systems. The regions also plan to expand electricity trade within Canada, and the governments will advocate for federal support that would include investment to accelerate transmission corridors.

Canada's grid was built for a different era, designed within provincial borders and not for a modern economy, Ontario Minister of Energy and Mines Stephen Lecce said. "This first-of-its-kind partnership lays the foundation for thousands of jobs, billions in investment, and a modernized grid," he said.

The federal government has taken steps over the last year, since the Trump administration imposed sweeping tariffs on goods imported into the U.S., to remove barriers to interprovincial trade and labor mobility in the country.

Goods and services worth more than 530 billion Canadian dollars, the equivalent of about US$387 billion, move across provincial and territorial borders each year, equal to almost 20% of Canada's gross domestic product. Ottawa points to research indicating that eliminating all federal, provincial and territorial internal trade barriers could boost GDP by as much as C$200 billion over time. A recent peer-reviewed study commissioned by Canada's Competition Bureau found that removing overly restrictive regulatory barriers in areas such as energy, transportation and retail distribution would boost economic growth by up to 10% over the long term.

Ontario's government said that by working to modernize and connect electric systems, provinces and territories will reduce duplication, lower longer-term costs and ensure Canada has the infrastructure needed to compete globally.

The province's Independent Electricity System Operator forecasts the province's demand for electricity will grow by as much 90% by 2050 after years of immigration-driven population growth and as Ontario pushes for industrial and mining developments. Ontario already operates 14 interties, or high-voltage lines interconnecting neighboring utility systems, three with Manitoba and 11 with Quebec.

Ontario and other provinces are already working to bolster their power-generation capacity to meet growing demand, and to run networks into remote areas that are underserved.

"This agreement recognizes the importance of electricity interties to Canada's strategic position in a rapidly changing world," Ted Laking, minister for energy, mines and resources in the northern Yukon territory, said.

Laking said a grid connection between Yukon and British Columbia to the south would provide needed reliable winter electricity to the territory, reduce diesel dependence in remote communities, and create partnership opportunities for First Nations. It also would help unlock significant critical-minerals development in the country's northwest and support Arctic sovereignty.


Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

Alau Tecnología S.A.U. announced that it has received $195 million in funding from Allianz X GmbH, Stone Ridge Holdings Group LP, Tencent Holdings Limited, Soros Fund Management LLC, D1 Capital Partners L.P., Table Holdings L.P.
data.description: Alau Tecnología S.A.U. announced that it has received $195,000,000 in a round of funding led by returning investor, Allianz X GmbH on March 4, 2026. The transaction included participation from new and returning investors, including returning investors, Stone Ridge Holdings Group LP, Tencent Holdings Limited, Soros Fund Management LLC, D1 Capital Partners L.P., and new inventor, Table Holdings L.P. The company has issued convertible preferred stock in the transaction. The round has been raised at post money valuation of $3,200,000,000.

U.S. Services-Sector Activity Continues to Rise in February -- ISM
data.description:
By Jessica Coacci

U.S. services-sector activity continued to rise in February, with all four subindexes being in expansion territory for the third month in a row, a monthly survey said.

The Institute for Supply Management's purchasing managers index for services providers was 56.1 in February compared with 53.8 in January. Economists polled by The Wall Street Journal expected a reading of 53.5. A reading above 50 suggests economic growth, while one below 50 indicates contraction.

"The services sector is heating up with business activity, new orders and new export orders indexes at their highest levels since 2024, and the backlog of orders index with its best reading since July 2022," said Steve Miller, chair of the ISM.

The employment index expanded for the third month in a row.

The prices index registered 63 in February and was below its 12-month average. February's reading is its lowest since March 2025, but has exceeded 60 for 15 straight months.

Gasoline was noted by some respondents as a commodity up in price for the first time since February 2025, and copper was up in price for the third month in a row, the survey said.

Commentary on trade uncertainty increased, with respondents commenting that tariffs effects have stabilized and are now embedded in supply-chain costs.

Fourteen industries reported growth in February, three more than in January, and the number reporting contraction shrank to three.

The data come after ISM reported earlier this week that manufacturing activity expanded in February for the second straight month as new orders and production continued to grow.

Write to Jessica Coacci at jessica.coacci@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

TSX rises on tech, mining strength
data.description:
March 4 (Reuters) - Canada's main stock index edged higher on Wednesday, lifted by mining and tech shares, while investors weighed a report that Iranian operatives reached out to the U.S. to pursue talks to end the conflict. 

The S&P/TSX composite index was up 0.33% at 33,896.21 points, as of 10:11 a.m. ET. The index nearly fell 4% in the previous session before recovering, as fears of inflation rippled across global markets.

"The market is expecting things to de-escalate. We've seen multiple global conflicts impacting the availability of oil over the last several years. Throughout all that, we've come through with a reasonably strong global economy, measured oil prices and good stock performance," said Josh Sheluk, portfolio manager at Verecan Capital Management.

Tech stocks led the sectoral gains with a 2.6% jump, as blockchain farm operator Bitfarms climbed 9% after bitcoin prices hit more than a three-week high. 

The gold index rose 0.5% as spot gold gained 1.4%. On the broader materials sector, base metal miners gained 0.6% as silver and copper prices also climbed. 

The heavily weighted financial index rose 0.5%.

Energy shares traded 0.6% lower as oil prices eased, retreating from gains made early in the day amid the Middle East conflict.

Iranian intelligence operatives indirectly contacted the CIA a day after the attacks, but U.S. officials remain skeptical that either the Trump administration or Iran is prepared for a near-term de-escalation, the New York Times reported.

President Donald Trump said on Tuesday the U.S. Navy could begin escorting tankers through the Strait of Hormuz if necessary.

Among individual movers, shares of pet-food retailer Pet Valu fell 4.4% after multiple brokerages cut its price target. The broader consumer discretionary subindex fell 0.2%. 

Shares of George Weston fell 3.1% after the food and drug retailer missed fourth-quarter revenue estimates, sending the consumer staples sector down 0.7%. 

(Reporting by Utkarsh Tushar Hathi; Editing by Shreya Biswas)

By Utkarsh Hathi

data.title: Johnson Controls International plc Announces Quarterly Dividend, Payable on April 10, 2026
data.description: The board of directors of Johnson Controls International plc has approved a regular quarterly dividend of $0.40 per share of common stock, payable on April 10, 2026, to shareholders of record at the close of business on March 16, 2026. Johnson Controls has paid a consecutive dividend since 1887.

data.title: Coherent Corp. Launches WaveMaker® 4000A for Super C-Band DWDM System Testing
data.description:
Coherent Corp. introduced the WaveMaker 4000A Programmable Optical Spectrum Synthesizer, designed to create D-WDM test spectra across the entire Super C-Band for transmission systems, transceivers, and amplifier test applications. The WaveMaker 4000A/Super C-Band is a compact, single-instrument solution that delivers arbitrarily programmable optical spectra across the full Super C-Band, supporting system testing for long-haul terrestrial and submarine networks as well as transceiver validation.

It generates high-power noise channels on programmable grids, including Flexgrid configurations, and enables defined optical noise loading of modulated channels to replicate real-world network conditions. The intuitive graphical user interface and programming interface allow seamless integration into automated test environments, while its compact footprint replaces rack-sized custom-built systems, saving valuable lab space. The WaveMaker 4000A/Super C-Band will be introduced at OFC 2026 in Los Angeles and is available for ordering with an eight-week lead time.

The instrument will be featured in a live demonstration at OFC 2026 at the Coherent booth #1401.

Adidas Israel Store Hit by Bomb -- Update
data.description:
By Andrea Figueras


Adidas said one of its stores in Israel was bombed and destroyed after the conflict in the Middle East escalated.

No employees were killed or hurt during the attack on the closed store, which took place three days ago, Chief Commercial Officer Mathieu Sidokpohou told reporters Wednesday.

Israel has in recent days been hit by missiles fired by Iran and militant group Hezbollah in retaliation for U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iranian targets, and for Israeli attacks on Lebanon. Other U.S. allies including the Gulf emirates have also been targeted by Tehran.

The company has roughly 3,000 employees in the six countries where it directly operates, which are currently impacted by the conflict, Sidokpohou said, noting that none have been injured or killed in the recent attacks.

The company also has franchise partners in other countries within the region. In total, Adidas operates 350 stores in the Middle East, 150 of which are directly owned.

Adidas Chief Executive Bjorn Gulden said sales in the region could be dragged by store closures due to the conflict, as well as by delays on some products due to shipment constraints. Gulden didn't put a number on the potential cost, noting that the situation could change at any time.

Swedish fashion retailer H&M, meanwhile, said it has closed all stores in Israel following local security directives.


Write to Andrea Figueras at andrea.figueras@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

Canada Services PMI Remains in Contraction Even as It Climbs to 46.5 in February
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart


Activity in Canada's services industry continued to weaken last month, with demand remaining weak and new orders falling, data released Wednesday showed.

The S&P Global Canada services purchasing managers index came in at 46.5 in February. That marked a fourth straight month below the 50 threshold between expansion and contraction, though it was a modest pickup after a slip to 45.8 in the first month of the year.

Services companies in the country continued to report a challenging operating environment, with clients showing uncertainty and a cautious attitude when committing to new business, the survey found.

"Canada's service-sector economy remained mired in a downturn during February, with both activity and new business falling again, albeit to weaker degrees," Paul Smith, economics director at S&P Global Market Intelligence, said.

The continued weakening in the services environment contrasts with manufacturing conditions in Canada, which have improved so far this year despite lingering concerns about trade and U.S. tariffs. S&P Global's purchasing managers index for the industry nudged up to 51 in February, marking a second straight month above the 50 mark following a year in contraction territory.

The Bank of Canada has projected soft economic growth ahead as supply chains are restructured and businesses adapt to the evolving global trade environment. Gross domestic product contracted 0.6% on an annualized basis in the final quarter of 2025, largely thanks to a big withdrawal from business inventories that offset growth in exports and increases in both household and government spending.

S&P Global's services survey showed the decline in new orders has now extended to 15 months, though the pace of the pullback in February was the softest since last October. Services providers reported a mixture of lower sales to both domestic and foreign clients, with new export business volumes declining again for the month.

A lack of new work meant companies on average either chose to not replace job leavers or were forced into layoffs during February, S&P Global said. Employment for the sector declined for a sixth successive month although, as with other variables measured by the survey, to a lesser degree than in January.

Even with reduced capacity, levels of work outstanding were reduced but companies continued to report that labor costs remained a key source of higher operating expenses in February. S&P Global said firms also noted a general increase in supplier charges, though input price inflation maintained its recent easing trend.

The survey showed that confidence in the outlook remained positive overall in February, with sentiment edging up to its highest level since October amid hopes that demand will pick up, though uncertainty persisted in the outlook, most notably in relation to trade and tariffs. Sentiment overall remained below trend, S&P Global said.


Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-04-26 1017ET

data.title: Rheinmetall Buys Majority Stake in Croatian Military Vehicles Manufacturer DOK-ING
data.description:
By Cristina Gallardo


Rheinmetall said it bought a majority stake in Croatian unmanned military vehicles manufacturer DOK-ING with the aim of expanding its drone portfolio.

The German defense group said Wednesday that it had acquired a 51% stake in DOK-ING, while its founder Vjekoslav Majetic will become the only other owner of the company, holding the remaining 49% stake.

The transaction will help Rheinmetall boost its portfolio of uncrewed and autonomous systems for military applications, and capitalize on DOK-ING's expertise on uncrewed solutions for mine clearance and operations in hazardous environments, the German company said.

Founded in 1991, DOK-ING will continue to be headquartered in Zagreb after this transaction, and preserve its management team, Rheinmetall said.

The combination of Rheinmetall's expertise in tactical vehicles and DOK-ING's capabilities in uncrewed systems will create a strong market position for Rheinmetall with considerable potential for the future, according to Bjorn Bernhard, chief of Rheinmetall's Vehicle Systems Europe division.

The move will also allow Rheinmetall to establish a foothold in Croatia, he said, describing the Balkan country as a "highly interesting customer."

Financial details for the transaction weren't disclosed.


Write to Cristina Gallardo at cristina.gallardo@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

Dassault Aviation Laments SCAF Program Delays… and Points Finger at Airbus
data.description:
Dassault Aviation has announced that the SCAF or FCAS (Future Combat Air System) program, intended to eventually replace both the Rafale and the Eurofighter, is experiencing progress difficulties due to disagreements among the partner companies.

Indeed, Paris and Berlin have different requirements for the future combat aircraft. France needs an aircraft capable of carrying nuclear warheads while remaining light enough to land on an aircraft carrier, whereas Germany would prefer a larger plane capable of carrying more weaponry.

During the presentation of the 2025 results, CEO Éric Trappier spoke of a project that is stalling and called for an end to further delays. He recalled that France had been designated in 2018 to lead the aircraft pillar of the program—known as "Pillar 1"—and that Dassault Aviation was therefore to act as the main contractor and leader.

Tensions have since arisen with Airbus Germany, which is advocating for co-leadership and fears being sidelined. "Either you are the leader, or you are not," the Dassault Aviation executive emphasized, adding that developing a next-generation combat aircraft requires a clearly identified industrial leader to make technological decisions and ensure system coherence.

When presenting its results, Guillaume Faury, head of Airbus, acknowledged ongoing deadlocks, linked, in his view, "to different expectations among the partners regarding governance and the definition of leadership and cooperation."

While the idea of a two-aircraft solution is beginning to take shape among some, the Dassault boss is clearly opposed, stating that France does not support this approach.

For his part, Guillaume Faury left the door open to this idea: "If our clients ask us, we will support a two-combat-aircraft solution and are committed to playing a leading role in the reorganization of FCAS, as a result of European cooperation."
As the current phase of the program is supposed to lead to the construction of a flying demonstrator, Éric Trappier warned that any delay in deciding to move to the next stage could impact the overall project timeline.

"I repeat, Airbus does not want to work with Dassault. I understand that—Airbus wants to work alone [...]. I do not know if they received a request from Germany that prompted these statements, but yes, this project is at a standstill. It is complicated," he said, before cautioning: "If Airbus does not want to work with Dassault, the project may not succeed."
